chunks.text: I do not believe that democracy necessarily leads to development. I believe that what a country needs to develop is discipline more than democracy. The exuberance of democracy leads to undisciplined and disorderly conditions which are inimical to development. I am not against the principles of democracy. But I am against the uncritical transfer of a system of government from one society to another, as if it were a universal formula that works everywhere regardless of history and culture. The ultimate test of the value of a political system is whether it helps that society to establish conditions which improve the standard of living for the majority of its people, plus enabling the maximum of personal freedoms compatible with the freedoms of others in society.

meta.title: Democracy, Discipline, and "Asian Values"
meta.work: Culture Is Destiny: A Conversation with Lee Kuan Yew
meta.source: Fareed Zakaria, 'Culture Is Destiny: A Conversation with Lee Kuan Yew', Foreign Affairs, March/April 1994.

chunks.text: e standard of living for the majority of its people, plus enabling the maximum of personal freedoms compatible with the freedoms of others in society. Eastern societies believe that the individual exists in the context of his family. He is not pristine and separate. The family is part of the extended family, and then friends and the wider society. The ruler or the government does not try to provide for a person what the family best provides. There is a little Confucianist concept that order in society, the harmony of relationships, comes before the rights of the individual.

meta.title: Democracy, Discipline, and "Asian Values"
meta.work: Culture Is Destiny: A Conversation with Lee Kuan Yew
meta.source: Fareed Zakaria, 'Culture Is Destiny: A Conversation with Lee Kuan Yew', Foreign Affairs, March/April 1994.

chunks.text: Bilingualism was one of the most difficult policies I had to push through, and one of the most important. English gave us access to the world, to science, technology, and commerce, and it was a neutral language that did not advantage any one of our races over the others. The mother tongue kept us anchored to our own culture and values, so that we would not become a pseudo-Western society, deculturalised and confused about our identity. I made mistakes along the way. For years I assumed that intelligence meant a child could master two languages equally well. I was wrong. Language ability and general intelligence are not the same thing. When I understood this, we changed the way we taught the mother tongue, to make it learnable rather than a source of failure.

meta.title: Bilingualism and the Politics of Language
meta.work: My Lifelong Challenge: Singapore's Bilingual Journey
meta.source: Lee Kuan Yew, 'My Lifelong Challenge: Singapore's Bilingual Journey' (Straits Times Press, 2012).

chunks.text: are not the same thing. When I understood this, we changed the way we taught the mother tongue, to make it learnable rather than a source of failure. A people who lose their own language and absorb only a foreign one lose a part of themselves. But a people who refuse the language of commerce and science condemn themselves to backwardness. We had to hold both, and that balance was hard-won.

meta.title: Bilingualism and the Politics of Language
meta.work: My Lifelong Challenge: Singapore's Bilingual Journey
meta.source: Lee Kuan Yew, 'My Lifelong Challenge: Singapore's Bilingual Journey' (Straits Times Press, 2012).

chunks.text: The size of China's displacement of the world balance is such that the world must find a new balance. It is not possible to pretend that this is just another big player. This is the biggest player in the history of the world. China wants to be China and accepted as China, not as an honorary member of the West. The Chinese will want to share this century as co-equals with the United States. They have a culture four thousand years old with 1.3 billion people, with a huge and very talented pool from which to draw. How could they not aspire to be number one in Asia, and in time the world?

meta.title: On the Rise of China
meta.work: One Man's View of the World
meta.source: Lee Kuan Yew, 'One Man's View of the World' (Straits Times Press, 2013), chapter on China.

chunks.text: 1.3 billion people, with a huge and very talented pool from which to draw. How could they not aspire to be number one in Asia, and in time the world? But China faces enormous problems: an aging population, a shrinking workforce, the lack of the rule of law, weak institutions, a language that is not the language of the internet, and the challenge of governing a continent-sized country from the centre. Will China become a parliamentary democracy? I do not think so, not in the form the West understands it. They will do it their own way. My advice to small countries caught between great powers is simple: do not take sides if you can avoid it; stay useful to both; and never make yourself so dependent on one that you lose your freedom of action.

meta.title: On the Rise of China
meta.work: One Man's View of the World
meta.source: Lee Kuan Yew, 'One Man's View of the World' (Straits Times Press, 2013), chapter on China.

chunks.text: The strongest deterrent is in a public opinion which censures and condemns corrupt persons; in other words, in attitudes which make corruption so unacceptable that the stigma of corruption cannot be washed away by serving a prison sentence. We made sure that the laws against corruption were enforced without fear or favour, starting from the top. We decided to pay our ministers and senior officials properly, comparable to what they could earn in the private sector. People said it was immoral to pay leaders so much. I said the opposite is immoral: to underpay them and then be surprised when they are tempted, or when the able ones leave for the private sector and you are left with the second-rate to run the country. Hypocrisy does not make for good government.

meta.title: Corruption, Clean Government, and Paying Ministers Well
meta.work: From Third World to First / parliamentary debates on ministerial salaries
meta.source: Lee Kuan Yew, 'From Third World to First' (2000), chapters on clean government; parliamentary debate on ministerial pay (1994).

chunks.text: when the able ones leave for the private sector and you are left with the second-rate to run the country. Hypocrisy does not make for good government. Clean, effective government is not the natural state of affairs. It has to be built and constantly defended. Once a society loses the habit of clean government, corruption spreads like gangrene, and it is extremely difficult to root out. We were determined that Singapore would not go that way.

meta.title: Corruption, Clean Government, and Paying Ministers Well
meta.work: From Third World to First / parliamentary debates on ministerial salaries
meta.source: Lee Kuan Yew, 'From Third World to First' (2000), chapters on clean government; parliamentary debate on ministerial pay (1994).

chunks.text: When the British announced they would withdraw their forces, we were a tiny island with no army worth the name. I said that Singapore had to be like a poisonous shrimp: small enough to be swallowed, but poisonous enough that whoever swallowed us would suffer for it. No one should imagine that they could take us cheaply. So we built up the Singapore Armed Forces from almost nothing, and we introduced National Service, requiring every able-bodied young man to serve and to train. A small country cannot hire others to defend it. Every citizen must be prepared to bear arms for his own home. Defence is not only soldiers and weapons; it is the will of a whole people to survive, what we came to call total defence: military, civil, economic, social, and psychological.

meta.title: Defence, National Service, and the "Poisonous Shrimp"
meta.work: Speeches on defence and National Service
meta.source: Compiled from Lee Kuan Yew's speeches on national defence and the introduction of National Service (1967); see 'From Third World to First' (2000).

chunks.text: rs and weapons; it is the will of a whole people to survive, what we came to call total defence: military, civil, economic, social, and psychological. A country that is not prepared to defend itself does not deserve to survive, and will not. We could not depend on the goodwill of others for our security. We had to make ourselves strong enough that aggression against us would never be worth the price.

meta.title: Defence, National Service, and the "Poisonous Shrimp"
meta.work: Speeches on defence and National Service
meta.source: Compiled from Lee Kuan Yew's speeches on national defence and the introduction of National Service (1967); see 'From Third World to First' (2000).

chunks.text: Deng Xiaoping was the most impressive leader I ever met. He was a great man because he changed China from a backward communist society that was going nowhere into the fastest-growing economy in the world. When he came to Singapore in 1978 and saw what we had done, a Chinese-majority society that was clean, green, and prosperous, he went back and set China on the path of reform and opening up. What struck me about Deng was that he was completely unsentimental and utterly practical. He famously said it did not matter whether a cat was black or white, so long as it caught mice. He was prepared to abandon ideology when ideology did not produce results. That is the mark of a serious leader: to face facts, to admit what is not working, and to change course without losing face or nerve.

meta.title: On Deng Xiaoping and China's Opening Up
meta.work: From Third World to First / One Man's View of the World
meta.source: Lee Kuan Yew, 'From Third World to First' (2000), on his meetings with Deng Xiaoping (1978); 'One Man's View of the World' (2013).

chunks.text: produce results. That is the mark of a serious leader: to face facts, to admit what is not working, and to change course without losing face or nerve. He told his people to seek truth from facts, and to cross the river by feeling for the stones. That is exactly how I have always tried to govern: not by theory or doctrine, but by testing what works, keeping what works, and discarding what does not.

meta.title: On Deng Xiaoping and China's Opening Up
meta.work: From Third World to First / One Man's View of the World
meta.source: Lee Kuan Yew, 'From Third World to First' (2000), on his meetings with Deng Xiaoping (1978); 'One Man's View of the World' (2013).

chunks.text: We were criticised for being a nanny state, for our fines on littering, on spitting, on failing to flush, for banning the import of chewing gum. If this is a nanny state, I am proud to have fostered one. We started off with people who behaved as people do when nobody is watching, and we ended up with a clean, green, orderly city that the world admires. I am often asked whether such rules infringe on personal freedom. My answer is that the freedom to throw your rubbish where you like, to foul the common space that belongs to everyone, is not a freedom worth defending. The chewing gum stuck on the train doors that stopped the trains running, the vandalism, the filth, these are not expressions of liberty; they are the failure of a society to discipline itself.

meta.title: Laws, Discipline, and the "Nanny State"
meta.work: Interviews and speeches on social discipline
meta.source: Compiled from Lee Kuan Yew's remarks defending Singapore's social discipline laws (littering, chewing gum, jaywalking); see 'From Third World to First' (2000).

chunks.text: at stopped the trains running, the vandalism, the filth, these are not expressions of liberty; they are the failure of a society to discipline itself. Some societies believe that you must let people do as they please and that order will somehow emerge. My experience is the opposite. Civilised living does not happen by accident. It is the result of standards set, taught, and enforced, generation after generation, until they become second nature.

meta.title: Laws, Discipline, and the "Nanny State"
meta.work: Interviews and speeches on social discipline
meta.source: Compiled from Lee Kuan Yew's remarks defending Singapore's social discipline laws (littering, chewing gum, jaywalking); see 'From Third World to First' (2000).

chunks.text: The accepted wisdom of development economists at the time was that multinationals were exploiters of cheap land, labour, and raw materials. This dependency school of thought was a great fashion in the 1970s. But by then we had already concluded that if Singapore was to survive, we had to attract the multinationals, learn from them, and use them to leapfrog the region. We had to create the conditions: political stability, sound macroeconomic management, an educated and disciplined workforce, infrastructure that worked, and absolute reliability in honouring contracts. We made it our business to be the most efficient, the cleanest, and the most predictable place to do business in Asia. When investors compared us with our neighbours, they had to conclude that Singapore was the safe bet.

meta.title: Economic Strategy — Multinationals and the First-World Oasis
meta.work: From Third World to First
meta.source: Lee Kuan Yew, 'From Third World to First' (2000), chapters on economic development strategy.

chunks.text: e most predictable place to do business in Asia. When investors compared us with our neighbours, they had to conclude that Singapore was the safe bet. Our strategy was to be a first-world oasis in a third-world region. If we could offer first-world standards of infrastructure, of security, of services, and of the rule of law, then businesses would base themselves here even though we were a small island, because we gave them what the region could not.

meta.title: Economic Strategy — Multinationals and the First-World Oasis
meta.work: From Third World to First
meta.source: Lee Kuan Yew, 'From Third World to First' (2000), chapters on economic development strategy.

chunks.text: A small country must seek a maximum number of friends, while maintaining the freedom to be itself as a sovereign and independent nation. Friendship, in international relations, is not a function of goodwill or personal affection. We must make ourselves relevant so that other countries have an interest in our continued survival and prosperity as a sovereign and independent nation. Poisonous though it may sound, in the relations between states, there are no permanent friends and no permanent enemies. There are only permanent interests. A small state must understand this clearly and never delude itself that sentiment will protect it. We must be useful, we must be principled, and we must be strong enough to make others pause before they harm us.

meta.title: The Foreign Policy of a Small State
meta.work: Speeches on foreign policy and regional diplomacy
meta.source: Compiled from Lee Kuan Yew's speeches on Singapore's foreign policy and the conduct of small states; see 'One Man's View of the World' (2013).

chunks.text: tself that sentiment will protect it. We must be useful, we must be principled, and we must be strong enough to make others pause before they harm us. We cannot change geography. Our neighbours are our neighbours forever. We have to live with them, trade with them, and find ways to manage our differences without illusions, neither subservient nor needlessly provocative.

meta.title: The Foreign Policy of a Small State
meta.work: Speeches on foreign policy and regional diplomacy
meta.source: Compiled from Lee Kuan Yew's speeches on Singapore's foreign policy and the conduct of small states; see 'One Man's View of the World' (2013).

chunks.text: After independence, I searched for some dramatic way to distinguish ourselves from other Third World countries. I settled on a clean and green Singapore. I started a programme to plant trees, millions of them, along every road and on every patch of bare earth. A blighted urban jungle of concrete destroys the human spirit. We needed the greenery of nature to lift up our people. I made greening the whole island a national priority. I personally pressed for it, demanded reports on it, and made ministers and officials accountable for it. When foreign investors and visitors landed and drove into the city through avenues of trees and well-kept gardens, they formed an instant impression: here is a country that is competent, that pays attention to detail, that takes care of its environment, and therefore will take care of their investment.

meta.title: The Garden City and a Clean, Green Singapore
meta.work: From Third World to First / speeches on greening Singapore
meta.source: Lee Kuan Yew, 'From Third World to First' (2000), chapter on greening Singapore and the Garden City vision.

chunks.text: e is a country that is competent, that pays attention to detail, that takes care of its environment, and therefore will take care of their investment. A clean and green environment was not a luxury we indulged in after we became rich. It was part of how we became rich. It signalled discipline, order, and pride, and it gave our people, however modest their means, a beautiful city to call home.

meta.title: The Garden City and a Clean, Green Singapore
meta.work: From Third World to First / speeches on greening Singapore
meta.source: Lee Kuan Yew, 'From Third World to First' (2000), chapter on greening Singapore and the Garden City vision.

chunks.text: The world does not stand still, and neither can we. The factories that once gave our workers jobs can move to China, to Vietnam, to wherever labour is cheaper. So we must keep moving up the ladder, into work that others cannot do as well: finance, research, design, high-value manufacturing, services that require skill and trust. The day we stop being useful is the day the investments and the jobs leave. In the old economy, you could prosper with your hands and your back. In the new economy, you prosper with your head. That is why we keep investing in education, in retraining, in lifelong learning. A worker today may have to change his trade two or three times in a working life. We must give our people the means to do that, or they will be left behind by a world that does not wait.

meta.title: Globalisation, the Knowledge Economy, and Staying Relevant
meta.work: National Day Rally and economic speeches
meta.source: Compiled from Lee Kuan Yew's speeches on globalisation, the knowledge economy, and Singapore's continued relevance; see 'Hard Truths' (2011).

chunks.text: s trade two or three times in a working life. We must give our people the means to do that, or they will be left behind by a world that does not wait. Globalisation rewards the able and punishes the complacent. For a small, open economy like ours, there is no shelter from these winds. Our only protection is to be better, faster, and more adaptable than the competition. Relevance is something you have to earn again every single day.

meta.title: Globalisation, the Knowledge Economy, and Staying Relevant
meta.work: National Day Rally and economic speeches
meta.source: Compiled from Lee Kuan Yew's speeches on globalisation, the knowledge economy, and Singapore's continued relevance; see 'Hard Truths' (2011).

chunks.text: I was convinced that if every family owned its home, the country would be more stable. After the 1965 separation, I was troubled by the prospect of young men with nothing to lose. If they had their own homes, their own stake in the country, they would defend it. A man who owns his home will fight to protect it. A man who rents has no such attachment. We made it a deliberate policy to give every citizen a tangible asset in the nation, and a sense that this country belongs to him. We used the Central Provident Fund savings to let people buy their flats, so that ownership was within reach of ordinary workers, not only the wealthy. Within a generation we became a nation of home owners. Ownership changes how a man thinks. It gives him dignity and a reason to care about his neighbourhood, his estate, and his country. This was not just economics; it was nation-building.

meta.title: Home Ownership and Giving Every Citizen a Stake
meta.work: From Third World to First
meta.source: Lee Kuan Yew, 'From Third World to First' (2000), chapters on housing and the Home Ownership Scheme.

chunks.text: India is not a real country. Instead it is thirty-two separate nations that happen to be arrayed along the British rail line. It is a civilisation of enormous depth and talent, but it is held back by its caste system, its bureaucracy, and the sheer difficulty of governing such diversity through a noisy, contentious democracy. I have great respect for the ability of individual Indians. Wherever they go, in the professions, in business, in science, they excel. The problem has never been the people; it has been the system, the layers of administration inherited from the British and multiplied since, that strangle initiative and slow everything down. When India finally cuts through its red tape and unleashes its people, it will be a formidable power.

meta.title: On India — Promise and Constraints
meta.work: One Man's View of the World
meta.source: Lee Kuan Yew, 'One Man's View of the World' (Straits Times Press, 2013), chapter on India.

chunks.text: at strangle initiative and slow everything down. When India finally cuts through its red tape and unleashes its people, it will be a formidable power. I do not believe India will grow as fast as China, because its politics will not allow the same decisiveness. But it will grow, and a strong India is good for the balance of Asia. The world is better with more than one centre of gravity.

meta.title: On India — Promise and Constraints
meta.work: One Man's View of the World
meta.source: Lee Kuan Yew, 'One Man's View of the World' (Straits Times Press, 2013), chapter on India.

chunks.text: Between being loved and being feared, I have always believed Machiavelli was right. If nobody is afraid of me, I am meaningless. When I say something, when I warn the people of consequences, they must know that I mean it and that there will be consequences. That is how you govern. I have never been overly concerned with popularity polls or opinion polls. I think a leader who is, is a weak leader. If you are concerned with whether your rating will go up or down, then you are not a leader. You are just chasing the wind. A leader must do what is right for the long term, even when it is not popular today. Whoever governs Singapore must have that iron in him. Or give it up. This is not a game of cards. This is your life and mine. I have spent a whole lifetime building this and as long as I am in charge, nobody is going to knock it down.

meta.title: On Leadership, Being Feared, and Popularity
meta.work: Various interviews and parliamentary speeches
meta.source: Compiled from Lee Kuan Yew's interviews and remarks on leadership, incl. 'One Man's View of the World' (2013) and parliamentary debates.

chunks.text: ame of cards. This is your life and mine. I have spent a whole lifetime building this and as long as I am in charge, nobody is going to knock it down. My job as a leader is to make sure that before the next elections, enough has developed and disclosed itself for the people to identify the duds, the failures, those who promised but never performed.

meta.title: On Leadership, Being Feared, and Popularity
meta.work: Various interviews and parliamentary speeches
meta.source: Compiled from Lee Kuan Yew's interviews and remarks on leadership, incl. 'One Man's View of the World' (2013) and parliamentary debates.

chunks.text: The most important thing I had to do was not to build roads or factories, but to find and groom the next generation of leaders. A leader who does not provide for his succession has failed, however brilliant his own record. I went out deliberately to recruit able, honest men and women into politics, often against their own inclinations, because I knew that the system we built would only last if better people kept coming up to run it. I stepped down as Prime Minister in 1990, while I still had the strength, precisely so that the transition would happen on my terms, in good order, and not in a crisis. Too many leaders cling to power until they are carried out, leaving chaos behind them. I wanted Singapore to learn the habit of orderly, peaceful renewal of leadership, generation after generation.

meta.title: Leadership Renewal and Stepping Down
meta.work: Speeches on self-renewal and the transition of power
meta.source: Compiled from Lee Kuan Yew's speeches on leadership self-renewal and his handover to Goh Chok Tong (1990); see 'From Third World to First' (2000).

chunks.text: carried out, leaving chaos behind them. I wanted Singapore to learn the habit of orderly, peaceful renewal of leadership, generation after generation. A nation's institutions must outlive the men who built them. If Singapore depends on any one man, then it is fragile, and it will not endure. My task was to make myself, in the end, unnecessary, to leave behind a system strong enough to carry on without me.

meta.title: Leadership Renewal and Stepping Down
meta.work: Speeches on self-renewal and the transition of power
meta.source: Compiled from Lee Kuan Yew's speeches on leadership self-renewal and his handover to Goh Chok Tong (1990); see 'From Third World to First' (2000).

chunks.text: I am not saying that everything I did was right, but everything I did was for an honourable purpose. I had to do some nasty things, locking fellows up without trial. But I was dealing with people who were determined to destroy what we were building, by violence and by subversion. I make no apologies for having defended Singapore by the means that were necessary. Even from my sickbed, even if you are going to lower me into the grave and I feel that something is going wrong, I will get up. Those who believe that when I have left the government as Senior Minister and Minister Mentor, I have gone into permanent retirement, are wrong. So long as I am able, so long as I have the strength, I will watch over this place.

meta.title: On His Legacy, Hard Choices, and Honour
meta.work: Hard Truths to Keep Singapore Going / late interviews
meta.source: Lee Kuan Yew, 'Hard Truths to Keep Singapore Going' (Straits Times Press, 2011), and late-career interviews.

chunks.text: Minister Mentor, I have gone into permanent retirement, are wrong. So long as I am able, so long as I have the strength, I will watch over this place. At the end of the day, what have I got? A successful Singapore. What have I given up? My life. I have lived my life in the service of this country, and I would do it again. Let history judge whether the choices were right. I am content that they were made for the right reasons.

meta.title: On His Legacy, Hard Choices, and Honour
meta.work: Hard Truths to Keep Singapore Going / late interviews
meta.source: Lee Kuan Yew, 'Hard Truths to Keep Singapore Going' (Straits Times Press, 2011), and late-career interviews.

chunks.text: Singapore's only resource is its people. We have no oil, no minerals, no land to speak of. The only asset we have is the ability and quality of our people. That is why we invest so heavily in education and in attracting talent. If we choose mediocre men to run the country, we will become a mediocre country. A nation is great not by its size alone. It is the will, the cohesion, the stamina, the discipline of its people, and the quality of their leaders which ensure it an honourable place in history. We must reward people according to their contribution and their merit, not according to their race, their connections, or their seniority. The moment we depart from merit, we begin to decline.

meta.title: Meritocracy, Talent, and Singapore's Only Resource
meta.work: Speeches on education and human capital
meta.source: Compiled from Lee Kuan Yew's speeches on education, meritocracy, and talent; see also 'From Third World to First' (2000).

chunks.text: ontribution and their merit, not according to their race, their connections, or their seniority. The moment we depart from merit, we begin to decline. We must always be open to talent. A small country cannot afford to close its doors. If you want to be a cosmopolitan, world-class city, you must welcome the able and the gifted, wherever they come from, and give them a stake in this place.

meta.title: Meritocracy, Talent, and Singapore's Only Resource
meta.work: Speeches on education and human capital
meta.source: Compiled from Lee Kuan Yew's speeches on education, meritocracy, and talent; see also 'From Third World to First' (2000).

chunks.text: I am not given to making sense out of life, or coming up with some grand narrative on it, other than to measure it by what you achieved. Life is just like that. As for me, I have done what I wanted to do, to the best of my ability. I am satisfied. I do not believe in an afterlife in the way the religions describe it. I have lived as a free thinker, weighing the evidence. When the end comes, I want it quick, without being a burden, without being kept alive by tubes when the mind has gone. I have made my wishes known: no heroic measures to prolong a life that has lost its meaning.

meta.title: On Mortality, Faith, and His Wife
meta.work: One Man's View of the World / late interviews
meta.source: Lee Kuan Yew, 'One Man's View of the World' (Straits Times Press, 2013), final chapter on life and death; late-career interviews following the death of Kwa Geok Choo (2010).

chunks.text: without being kept alive by tubes when the mind has gone. I have made my wishes known: no heroic measures to prolong a life that has lost its meaning. My wife and I were together for over sixty years. When she lay dying, unable to move, I would sit by her and read to her, the poems she loved, because the doctors said her hearing was the last to go. Grief is the price we pay for love. I do not regret the price. If I had to do it again, I would marry her again, and I would live my life in the service of Singapore again.

meta.title: On Mortality, Faith, and His Wife
meta.work: One Man's View of the World / late interviews
meta.source: Lee Kuan Yew, 'One Man's View of the World' (Straits Times Press, 2013), final chapter on life and death; late-career interviews following the death of Kwa Geok Choo (2010).

chunks.text: I said that our better-educated women were not marrying and not having enough children, while the less-educated were having more. If we continued like this, the quality of our population would suffer over the generations. It was an unpopular thing to say, and it cost us votes, but I believed it was true and that it needed saying. In hindsight, I pushed too hard and too bluntly, and I underestimated how deeply people resent the state telling them whom to marry and how many children to have. These are matters of the heart, not of policy. You can build the conditions, the housing, the incentives, the childcare, but you cannot order people to fall in love and to have babies. That much I learned.

meta.title: Population, Marriage, and the Limits of Social Engineering
meta.work: National Day Rally ("Great Marriage Debate") and later reflections
meta.source: Lee Kuan Yew, National Day Rally speech, 1983 (the 'Great Marriage Debate'); later reflections in 'Hard Truths' (2011).

chunks.text: build the conditions, the housing, the incentives, the childcare, but you cannot order people to fall in love and to have babies. That much I learned. A society that does not reproduce itself is a society in slow decline. No amount of immigration fully substitutes for a people who renew themselves. This remains, to my mind, one of the hardest long-term problems facing Singapore, and one for which there is no easy or popular answer.

meta.title: Population, Marriage, and the Limits of Social Engineering
meta.work: National Day Rally ("Great Marriage Debate") and later reflections
meta.source: Lee Kuan Yew, National Day Rally speech, 1983 (the 'Great Marriage Debate'); later reflections in 'Hard Truths' (2011).

chunks.text: The freedom of the press, the freedom of the news media, must be subordinated to the overriding needs of the integrity of Singapore, and to the primacy of purpose of an elected government. I do not accept that a newspaper proprietor or a foreign correspondent has the right to set the national agenda, or to inflame the passions of one community against another. In a young multiracial nation, the press can build up or pull down. It can stir up communal hatreds and undo in a week what took years to build. I have seen what an irresponsible press did to fan racial and religious fires elsewhere. So I make no apology for insisting that the press in Singapore play a constructive role, report fairly, and not become the instrument of any faction, local or foreign.

meta.title: The Role of the Press in a Developing Nation
meta.work: Address to the International Press Institute, Helsinki
meta.source: Lee Kuan Yew, address to the General Assembly of the International Press Institute, Helsinki, 9 June 1971 (National Archives of Singapore).

chunks.text: ogy for insisting that the press in Singapore play a constructive role, report fairly, and not become the instrument of any faction, local or foreign. I am not against criticism. Criticism that is fair, that is based on facts, that helps us govern better, is welcome. But criticism that is designed to mislead, to score points, or to serve a hidden agenda, I will answer, and answer robustly. The journalist is not the elected representative of the people; the government is.

meta.title: The Role of the Press in a Developing Nation
meta.work: Address to the International Press Institute, Helsinki
meta.source: Lee Kuan Yew, address to the General Assembly of the International Press Institute, Helsinki, 9 June 1971 (National Archives of Singapore).

chunks.text: Race and religion are the most visceral and dangerous fault lines in any society, and doubly so in ours, where Chinese, Malays, Indians, and others live cheek by jowl. I have seen the racial riots, the blood on the streets in the 1960s. I never forgot it. Everything we did in housing, in schools, in the law, was designed so that those fires could never be lit again. That is why we required every public housing estate to reflect the racial mix of the nation, so that no community could close itself off into a ghetto and breed suspicion of the others. That is why we kept religion out of politics and politics out of the pulpit. People may worship as they please, but no one may use his religion as a weapon against another community, or to claim that his faith should govern those who do not share it.

meta.title: Managing Race and Religion in a Multiracial Society
meta.work: Speeches on racial and religious harmony
meta.source: Compiled from Lee Kuan Yew's speeches on multiracialism, the ethnic integration policy, and religious harmony; see 'From Third World to First' (2000).

chunks.text: hey please, but no one may use his religion as a weapon against another community, or to claim that his faith should govern those who do not share it. Tolerance is not enough. We aimed for something more: to give every community a fair stake and a fair chance, so that each could see that its future lay in this shared nation, not in itself alone. Harmony between the races is not a natural condition. It has to be worked at, deliberately, every day, and it can be lost in a single careless generation.

meta.title: Managing Race and Religion in a Multiracial Society
meta.work: Speeches on racial and religious harmony
meta.source: Compiled from Lee Kuan Yew's speeches on multiracialism, the ethnic integration policy, and religious harmony; see 'From Third World to First' (2000).

chunks.text: I started off believing all men were equal. I now know that is the most unlikely thing ever to have been, because millions of years have passed over evolution, people have scattered across the face of this earth, been isolated from each other, developed independently, had different intermixtures between races, peoples, climates, soils. But one thing is certain: a society works only when there is order, and order rests on law that is applied equally to all. Human beings, regrettable though it may be, are inherently vicious and have to be restrained from their viciousness. People need to fear the consequences of wrongdoing. If you talk to a robber and reason with him about the social causes of his crime while he holds a knife to your throat, you will lose your wallet and possibly your life. First you establish order; then you can talk about everything else.

meta.title: Rule of Law, Order, and Social Discipline
meta.work: Speeches and interviews on law, order, and discipline
meta.source: Compiled from Lee Kuan Yew's remarks on the rule of law, order, and the social compact; see 'From Third World to First' (2000).

chunks.text: he holds a knife to your throat, you will lose your wallet and possibly your life. First you establish order; then you can talk about everything else. We were criticised for our laws, our caning, our restrictions. But a clean, safe, orderly city, where a woman can walk home alone at night, where children can go to school without fear, is not a small thing. It is the foundation on which everything else is built.

meta.title: Rule of Law, Order, and Social Discipline
meta.work: Speeches and interviews on law, order, and discipline
meta.source: Compiled from Lee Kuan Yew's remarks on the rule of law, order, and the social compact; see 'From Third World to First' (2000).

chunks.text: For me, it is a moment of anguish. All my life, my whole adult life, I have believed in merger and the unity of these two territories. You know that we, as a people, are connected by geography, economics, and ties of kinship. Would you mind if we stop for a while? Every time we look back on this moment when we signed this agreement which severed Singapore from Malaysia, it will be a moment of anguish. For we are a people who believe in the unity of these two territories. But Singapore will survive. We will build a multiracial nation. This is not a country that belongs to any single community; it belongs to all of us. We are going to have a multiracial nation in Singapore. We will set the example. This is not a Malay nation; this is not a Chinese nation; this is not an Indian nation. Everybody will have his place, equal: language, culture, religion.

meta.title: On the Separation of Singapore from Malaysia
meta.work: Television and radio press conference
meta.source: Lee Kuan Yew, press conference announcing Singapore's separation from Malaysia, 9 August 1965 (National Archives of Singapore).

chunks.text: We knew that if we were just like our neighbours, we would die. We had to be more disciplined, more organised, and more efficient than the rest of the region. If we were only as good as our neighbours, there was no reason for the world to do business with us. We had to make ourselves relevant to the world. Nobody owes Singapore a living. We are not a natural country but man-made. We had no hinterland, no resources, not even our own water. The world does not owe us a living and we cannot live by the begging bowl. We survive only because we offer something the others do not: a level of reliability, of trust, of competence, of safety, that makes it worth their while to come here.

meta.title: Survival, Vulnerability, and Nobody Owes Us a Living
meta.work: From Third World to First / National Day Rally speeches
meta.source: Lee Kuan Yew, 'From Third World to First: The Singapore Story 1965–2000' (2000), and National Day Rally addresses.

chunks.text: ecause we offer something the others do not: a level of reliability, of trust, of competence, of safety, that makes it worth their while to come here. We started with a small domestic market and high unemployment. The conventional wisdom of the time said a small country like ours could not industrialise. We ignored it. We went out and persuaded the multinationals to manufacture in Singapore when others shunned them. We made ourselves an oasis of first-world standards in a third-world region.

meta.title: Survival, Vulnerability, and Nobody Owes Us a Living
meta.work: From Third World to First / National Day Rally speeches
meta.source: Lee Kuan Yew, 'From Third World to First: The Singapore Story 1965–2000' (2000), and National Day Rally addresses.

chunks.text: I would not write off the United States. Their society is enormously creative because they reward enterprise and they tolerate failure. America's strength lies in its ability to attract talent from all over the world, to absorb them, and to give them the chance to do their best. No other society does this as well. The Americans have a culture of can-do, a belief that every problem can be solved with enough effort, money, and ingenuity. This optimism, this restlessness, is a great source of strength. They are not held back by tradition in the way old societies are. When they fail, they pick themselves up and try again.

meta.title: On the United States and Its Resilience
meta.work: One Man's View of the World
meta.source: Lee Kuan Yew, 'One Man's View of the World' (Straits Times Press, 2013), chapter on America.

chunks.text: s a great source of strength. They are not held back by tradition in the way old societies are. When they fail, they pick themselves up and try again. America will remain the pre-eminent power in Asia and the world for some decades to come. But the unipolar moment is over. The United States will have to learn to share power with China, and to accommodate a world in which it is no longer able to decide outcomes by itself. The question is whether it can do so gracefully.

meta.title: On the United States and Its Resilience
meta.work: One Man's View of the World
meta.source: Lee Kuan Yew, 'One Man's View of the World' (Straits Times Press, 2013), chapter on America.

chunks.text: We have to teach people that nobody owes them a living. The moment you give people something for nothing, and they come to expect it as a right, you have weakened them. Watching the welfare states of Europe and America, I concluded that their generous unemployment benefits and welfare payments had sapped the will to work and to strive. They had created a crutch mentality, where people leaned on the state instead of standing on their own feet. Our approach was different. We would help a man to help himself: subsidise his education, his housing, his health care, so that he and his children had a fair chance to rise by their own effort. But we would not pay him to be idle. We made it our principle that work, not welfare, was the path to dignity. The safety net should catch those who genuinely cannot cope, not become a hammock for those who will not try.

meta.title: Welfare, the "Crutch Mentality", and Self-Reliance
meta.work: From Third World to First / National Day Rally speeches
meta.source: Lee Kuan Yew, 'From Third World to First' (2000), chapters on social policy; National Day Rally addresses on welfare and self-reliance.

chunks.text: k, not welfare, was the path to dignity. The safety net should catch those who genuinely cannot cope, not become a hammock for those who will not try. If you reward the man who does not work the same as the man who works hard, you will soon find that nobody wants to do the hard, dirty, or unpleasant jobs. The incentive to excel disappears. A society that does this to itself is digging its own grave, however compassionate its intentions.

meta.title: Welfare, the "Crutch Mentality", and Self-Reliance
meta.work: From Third World to First / National Day Rally speeches
meta.source: Lee Kuan Yew, 'From Third World to First' (2000), chapters on social policy; National Day Rally addresses on welfare and self-reliance.

chunks.text: I have never believed that a society can be run on the basis that every individual does as he pleases and calls it freedom. The Western model assumes that the individual, with his rights, is the basic unit of society. We do not believe that. We believe the family is the basic unit, and that the interests of the community must temper the appetites of the individual.

meta.title: Western Liberalism, Human Rights, and Cultural Difference
meta.work: Interviews and speeches responding to Western critics
meta.source: Compiled from Lee Kuan Yew's interviews and speeches on Western liberalism and human rights; see 'The Wit and Wisdom of Lee Kuan Yew' (2013) and Foreign Affairs (1994).

chunks.text: We do not believe that. We believe the family is the basic unit, and that the interests of the community must temper the appetites of the individual. The Americans believe their system is the best for everyone, and that they have a duty to spread it. I have told them: do not impose your system on societies for which it was not designed and where it will not work. What use is a free press, a free vote, and endless litigation, if the result is a society that cannot feed itself, cannot keep order in its streets, and cannot educate its children? First things first: food, housing, health, education, security. Then, as a society matures, it can afford more of the freedoms the West takes for granted.

meta.title: Western Liberalism, Human Rights, and Cultural Difference
meta.work: Interviews and speeches responding to Western critics
meta.source: Compiled from Lee Kuan Yew's interviews and speeches on Western liberalism and human rights; see 'The Wit and Wisdom of Lee Kuan Yew' (2013) and Foreign Affairs (1994).

chunks.text: t things first: food, housing, health, education, security. Then, as a society matures, it can afford more of the freedoms the West takes for granted. I am not arguing that there are no universal values. There are. But the balance between the rights of the individual and the needs of society is struck differently in different cultures, and it is arrogant to assume that one society's balance must be every society's.

meta.title: Western Liberalism, Human Rights, and Cultural Difference
meta.work: Interviews and speeches responding to Western critics
meta.source: Compiled from Lee Kuan Yew's interviews and speeches on Western liberalism and human rights; see 'The Wit and Wisdom of Lee Kuan Yew' (2013) and Foreign Affairs (1994).
